ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific nasi lemak rice entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226355009 'Cooked rice' used as closest parent; no specific nasi lemak rice concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of nasi lemak rice is approximately 100 g of cooked coconut-milk rice, equivalent to roughly half a cup of steamed rice as typically served at a Singapore hawker stall or coffeeshop.

nutrient_source.title: Nasi lemak (rice) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo lists nasi lemak rice as a distinct entry. Values below are drawn from the HPB Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food database for cooked nasi lemak rice (rice component). Where HPB reports the full nasi lemak dish, the rice-only component values are used; these are consistent with published HPB per-100 g figures for coconut-milk steamed rice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for nasi lemak rice (coconut-milk steamed white rice) was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 54 is derived from the closest analogous entry: white rice cooked with coconut milk, which is reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). A GI of 54 is classified as 'low'. Coconut milk fat content slightly lowers the GI relative to plain boiled white rice (GI ~72) due to fat-mediated slowing of gastric emptying. GL per serving = (54 × 27.0 g carbohydrate × 1.0 serving weight 100 g) / 100 = 14.6.

hypertension.guidance: Nasi lemak rice contains approximately 120 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium intake across the full nasi lemak meal (including sambal, anchovies, and egg), which can collectively exceed 600–800 mg sodium per serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load and refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 54 (classified as low) and a glycemic load of ~14.6 per 100 g serving, nasi lemak rice has a relatively lower glycaemic impact compared to high-GI foods; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should still be mindful of portion size (100 g or less) and pair with high-fibre or protein-rich accompaniments to manage postprandial glucose excursions, as the overall meal can still be carbohydrate-dense.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Nasi lemak rice derives most of its fat from coconut milk, contributing approximately 3.8 g saturated fat per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of cumulative saturated fat intake, particularly when nasi lemak rice is consumed alongside other coconut-based or fried accompaniments.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nasi lemak rice is the fragrant coconut-milk steamed rice that forms the centrepiece of nasi lemak, one of Singapore's and Malaysia's most iconic dishes. The rice is cooked by simmering or steaming white jasmine or long-grain rice in a mixture of coconut milk, water, pandan (screwpine) leaves, and salt, which imparts a rich, creamy flavour and a subtly sweet, floral aroma. The cooked grains are slightly stickier and more flavourful than plain steamed rice, with a soft, tender texture and a pale ivory colour. In Singapore hawker culture, nasi lemak rice is typically served as a base portion accompanied by sambal chilli, fried anchovies (ikan bilis), roasted peanuts, cucumber slices, and a hard-boiled or fried egg. The rice component alone provides approximately 160 kcal, 27 g carbohydrate, 4.5 g fat (predominantly saturated fat from coconut milk), and 2.9 g protein per 100 g. Its glycaemic index is estimated at 54 (low), lower than plain boiled white rice due to the fat content of coconut milk slowing gastric emptying. Clinically, the dish is relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (low-to-moderate glycaemic load, depending on portion size and accompaniments), hypertension (sodium accumulates across accompaniments), and cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from coconut milk). Nasi lemak is a culturally significant dish in Singapore, Malaysia, and the broader Malay Archipelago, and is consumed at breakfast, lunch, and dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. The food is a well-defined, commonly measured dish component in the HPB database. GI is estimated from the closest analogous entry in the University of Sydney GI Database with documented rationale.
notes: Nutrient values represent the rice component of nasi lemak only, not the full dish with accompaniments. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — coconut milk contains no industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring trans fats in coconut are negligible and reported as zero in HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — rice and coconut milk contain no dietary cholesterol). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — cooked rice and coconut milk contain no meaningful vitamin C). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. GI of 54 (classified as low) is an analogous estimate based on coconut-milk cooked white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for nasi lemak rice was located. Serving size of 100 g is used as the standard portion for per-serving GL calculation. Allergen note: coconut is classified as a tree nut allergen in some jurisdictions (including Singapore's SFA allergen labelling framework).
